_meta.title: Minimum Spanning Trees — Prim, Kruskal, Borůvka

questions.question: According to the cut property, which statement is true for any cut (S, V\S) in a connected weighted graph?
options: The heaviest edge crossing the cut can never be in any MST
The lightest edge crossing the cut belongs to every MST
Exactly half the crossing edges belong to some MST
No edge crossing the cut belongs to the MST
questions.explanation: The cut property states that for any partition of vertices, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut is a safe edge — it belongs to every MST. This is proved by contradiction: if a lighter crossing edge existed outside some MST T, adding it creates a cycle, and removing the heavier crossing edge from that cycle yields a lighter spanning tree.

questions.question: What is the amortized time complexity per operation for Union-Find with both path compression and union by rank?
options: O(log n)
O(1) worst case
O(α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function
O(n)
questions.explanation: Tarjan proved (1975) that Union-Find with path compression and union by rank achieves O(α(n)) amortized time per operation. The inverse Ackermann function grows so slowly that it is ≤ 4 for any practical n (< 10^80 atoms in the universe). This is why Kruskal's sorting step — not the Union-Find — dominates the runtime.

questions.question: For a sparse graph with |E| ≈ |V|, which algorithm is typically faster in practice and why?
options: Kruskal's, because O(E log E) ≈ O(V log V) with very small constants
Prim's with a binary heap, because O(E log V) ≈ O(V log V) with lower constant overhead
Borůvka's, because it needs only O(log V) phases with no sorting step
All three are identical in this regime
questions.explanation: When |E| ≈ |V|, Kruskal's sorts only ~V edges (O(V log V)) and then does ~V nearly-O(1) Union-Find operations. Prim's does ~V heap operations (also O(V log V)) but with higher constant factors from heap maintenance. For very sparse graphs, Kruskal's simplicity and cache-friendly sorted-edge scan make it the practical winner. Prim's shines on dense graphs where |E| ≈ |V|², especially with an adjacency matrix and O(V²) scan instead of a heap.

questions.question: Why is Borůvka's algorithm particularly well-suited for parallel and distributed MST computation?
options: It uses a randomized pivot that can be sampled independently
Each component finds its lightest outgoing edge independently in a single phase — no global coordination needed
It avoids Union-Find entirely, making thread synchronization trivial
It only needs O(V) total work regardless of edge count
questions.explanation: In each Borůvka phase, every connected component independently scans its edges to find the lightest outgoing one. These searches have no data dependencies between components, so they can run in parallel across threads or machines. After the phase, components merge — a global synchronization barrier. Each phase at least halves the number of components, giving O(log V) phases and O(E log V) total work with high parallelism.

questions.question: What cut property do MST algorithms exploit?
options: The cheapest edge crossing any cut must be in some MST
The most expensive edge in any cycle must be in some MST
Every vertex must have exactly one incoming edge
The graph must be bipartite
questions.explanation: The cut property states that for any partition (cut) of vertices, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut belongs to some MST. Both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms are built on this property — Kruskal implicitly considers cuts defined by growing components.
